sentences.text: Well, you were telling us about your experiences in Auschwitz.

[SIGHS] The eeriness of those lights at-- all night, and when I had to go to use the facilities, that, that unbelievable strangeness of the lights, the electric wires, the fences, and the men with the-- with the guns in the lookout, and the, the people who couldn't take it anymore and flung themselves against the electric fence-- it's there always.
It is a picture you can not take out from your memory.

And there are times when I look at night-- if I get up and I go to the window and I look out, I have this mixture of feelings looking on Rutledge Road under light and seeing, in the back of my mind, Auschwitz.
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's, it's something-- and then the morning, to discuss with everybody who it was that did that.
And the fear of-- and the hopelessness and helplessness has, has embedded itself very, very deeply.
And then every single day that renewed attrition, punishment, imprinting the mind with fear, and seeing that people who are not feeling well taken to the infirmary, never to be seen again.
When I was-- I had pneumonia.
And they smuggled out aspirins from the infirmary because my-- the friends, my sister-- they would never permit me to go to the infirmary.
And they-- and they-- and I, I just don't understand with being as, as frail as I was-- because I just was a skinny child-- and having pneumonia and fainting regularly, and I'm here.
I say, well, this is-- this is something that is very difficult to explain.
How did I get through this?
And-- How do you explain it?
I, I just-- I just think that explanations are not good enough.
You cannot make a judgment on this and say 'this and this' is the reason.
I blocked a lot.
I imagine that when I am up against the wall, I block.
I do what's logical.
I don't give up.
I'm a fighter.
When someone needs me, I'm there.
And if-- I don't-- I just don't-- I don't make it into a, a romantic explanation or this or that.
It's just that when you see an anthill and you take a boot and you crush and you take your boot away, there are still ants come out from ant hill that you have crushed.
This is what happened to us.
We were the ants that the boot didn't crush.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why?
This is-- this is the nature's way of-- not nature.
This is the way it happens.
Did you wonder while you were there why this was happening to you?
Hatred of the Jews was so embedded in my mind from early childhood that when they were doing this, it was, well, the hatred did that.
So hatred is the cause of-- but now I know it's not only hatred.
It's religion that makes you hate.
Any time when another person isn't as good as you because you don't believe what they believe, that is the cause of, of human bad-- poor behavior.
Well, all those-- all those happenings that were happening daily.
Oh, once there was something very interesting.
And we were being counted.
And the SS woman has a--
No.
I just remember her fiery, brown, dagger-throwing eyes.
And she looked at you.
She was-- she once whipped me for having a-- for having a, a sweater, or something that-- I don't know why she did it.
But she hit me once.
She gave the bread.
And she stopped in our line-- lane.
She stopped.
And then she went for more bread.
And she started again with our line, in our lane.
Therefore we had twice bread.
And, of course, we quickly divided it.
And then there was one bread missing.
And they were giving us a very hard time, carrying on-- who got the bread?
And we no longer could say it is us because then the whole lane would have been shot right then and there.
So we had to-- we had the bread.
We couldn't take it, show it.
And we didn't know what-- what's going to happen.
Well, it-- they-- perhaps she thought that she miscounted.
And another bread-- after a long, long screaming session and fear-- it came out.
So that day, I had bread twice.
And that was the one and only happening that was very difficult but it was-- [LAUGHS] it was sustaining.
And then one day, we were marched out from Auschwitz from the electric fence.
We were out of the gate.
And we were going someplace.

And while we were in Auschwitz, we always had the, the big-- the furnace, the chimney, which was a fairly low chimney which is-- resembled what they have in the atomic power stations, that chunky, low, short kind of a chimney.
And that was always on.
And the ashes were flying all over.
And we had body-- we had ashes of bodies settle on all us, on-- when the wind blew that way.
Well, now we are going out.
And we are going towards a building.
We get there, and they tell us, get undressed.
And then we ushered-- we were ushered into a big room with little holes in the ceiling.
And they tell us we're going to be showered.
And somebody said, this is not a shower.
This is a gas chamber.
And we wait there over an hour.
And no, no water and no gas is coming.
And after a while, the doors fling open, the SS come in and start to separate people.
Again, to see who stuck with whom-- that they are sisters, they should be separated.
And who is-- who, who is close.
And my sister told me, go over to the other end right away.
And don't look at me.
And I did.
And they separated some.
And, and they let us stay in an other room where there were bleachers and a window, a one-way mirror.
Overnight we were there.
And next day we were marched back.
But on our way in, we have noticed a little bundle of eyeglasses, false teeth, watches, little items that were from people's belongings.
And my sister says, we are not supposed to see this.
There is something that is, is going to happen.
We're not allowed to-- this is not for us.
And on the way back, we saw them again in the morning.
And then we knew.
We were in the gas chamber, but they didn't have the cyanide.
The inventory was not correct.
And instead of taking the machine gun and get us-- eliminate us, the orders were not such.
So we went back.
And they-- you know, that time, I don't-- again, I must have been blocking because I didn't allow myself the panic.
So I'm going to die.
So, what can happen?
I'm going to die.
And, and I just blocked.
I blocked that.
And I was speaking to Helen [NON-ENGLISH].
And she says, I was there the same time you were.
She says, I had the same experience.
I said, what you were there?
Well, of course I didn't.
She says, I was there.
It happened to me.
I said, oh gosh that's what happened to you?
We were the same time there.
Who was Helen [NON-ENGLISH]?
Helen [NON-ENGLISH] lives in Queens.
And when-- I was talking about it, and, and she says, I was there.
That happened to me.
And it was very interesting that we-- that were maybe about 100 people, 150.
They-- from--
--both sides, they were holding me.
Well, we were close with the people.
We were supportive of each other.
And there was my-- there was a [NON-ENGLISH] and a [NON-ENGLISH].
And these were from my street.
We were living in the same area.
And there were this [NON-ENGLISH]-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what's her name-- a [NON-ENGLISH] girl.
And so we were a bunch of us that were supportive of each other.
And, as I said, that they took a little bit of my-- a sip of my soup.
And they gave me a quarter of their bread.
So they, they were all helping me survive.
They were--
They were all--
--all vicious, wild dogs.
Now they have made-- the, the Jewish people they have made as kapos.
Another punishment-- to get the Jewish, who-- they said, well, you will oversee these people.
You will be the, the kapo.
You have heard of 'kapos' word?
And after the war, these kapos were eliminated.
Whoever could, they were put into jail because it was the wrong thing.
They were-- they behaved very poorly.
And, and they were trying to get favor from the Germans by being very good to them and mean to us.
And they, they were around.
And they were overseeing that we should not take any liberties in any which way.
No, no, no.
We were in Auschwitz from April till, I think, the end of November.
And then the Russians were advancing-- is what we heard.
Well, also, I have a method is that while we were in Auschwitz, we were handed postcards to send to people we know that we are-- it was pre-written-- that we are well and, and regards.
That was-- that's what we did.
We also heard-- I don't know how, how this came about, that the Russians were advancing.
And then we were herded.
And we marched for days and days.
Every day, we were marching away from Auschwitz.
We were not put into any cars.
Well, towards-- away from the Russian front.
And then, on the way, whoever couldn't keep up was shot.
And the food was-- I don't-- I don't know.
I can't remember getting food.
I can't remember.
So I remember that my, my knees were locking.
And I, I-- it wasn't any more a, a, a reflex.
I couldn't put my foot out in front of me unless I thought very carefully to put it out.
I couldn't walk anymore.
And we sat overnight someplace.
And the next day, again.
And many, many people died on that march.
And then we were taken to a, a munition factory, to Hainichen.
Hainichen.
H-E-I-N-E-C-H-E-N.
I saw a documentary about Hainichen a half a year ago.
And I said, oh my god.
This is the place where I was.
There was a, a, a big building they have allocated for these prisoners.
And we worked in an ammunition factory 12 hours, either at nighttime or daytime.
They exchanged it.
[SIGHS] When we got there, there were Polish people in that camp already.
And there we already got some clothes.
And our shoes was already worn out.
The ones we came into with-- to Auschwitz was already-- it-- it's-- it was all used.
The leather sole was gone.
And they would hand us a pair which wasn't much good either.
And then we were given, once a day, food.
And we went to work.
I-- this man has noticed that I handle the machine well.
He noticed an, a-- an agility in me.
And he made me the [GERMAN], the forelady of that department.
We were about seven girls.
My--
Not quite.
No, I was-- not quite.
I was 15.
And then I got this job.
And I did the precision work with the-- drilling bits for precision work, making-- sharpening drill bits.
And at night, one time, I was just so tired.
And I had an awful lot to do.
They gave me a, a, a basketful-- a box full of things to sharpen.
And I dropped the main head on my-- of the-- of the machine on my toe.
And it broke.
[LAUGHS] My toe broke.
And they marched me back to the-- to the house-- to the building where we lived.
And there, I stayed in the infirmary for a few days.
And, of course, they-- you can't do anything with a broken toe.
And so then I was back at work.
And he tried to get me to read the, the manuals so that he could give me more intricate work.
But it didn't get to that.
He told me.
Go to the library and take out this book.
And he let me go alone to the library.
So there we were prisoners.
And they did not do the harassing as badly.
But for us, coming from Auschwitz, there was already a little bit eased up on the punishment.
But they did their beatings.
And they did-- they-- we didn't have enough food.
And we didn't have heat or anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were-- the Americans eventually started to bomb the, the factory.
But they must have had information because they didn't bomb the area we were.
When we-- on Sunday, they bombed the area where we were-- we were working during the week.
So they must have had information.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then eventually, we were taken from there into wagons, again into a cattle car, and-- because the Russians were advancing.
And we were taken to Theresienstadt.
And the trip there was another harrowing-- because we had no food.
And they took along beans and peas-- dried beans and peas in a kettle.
And that wasn't-- you can't cook that.
When you-- when you-- when you were trying to go away from the enemy, you can't stop and soak it and cook it.
So we were hungry.
And the SS, of course, were doing their punitive dealings with us.
And then we were bombed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were on the siding, and they were bombing.
And we-- of course, they dropped the bombs right above us.
And my sister told me, don't worry.
When they drop it right above you, it goes a kilometer and a half further.
So we will be safe.
And that's what happened.
And then we arrived to Theresienstadt.
And there already the, the gas chamber was waiting.
The inmates built the gas chamber for all those who were arriving from these places to definitely get rid of them.
And they-- we, we got the word that this and this-- count the bricks.
Such and such a number is not mortared.
It's not cemented in.
So you push it out-- when you're there and it comes, push it.
When the gas comes, push that wall out.
And they-- meanwhile, they were telling the-- telegraphing because in Theresienstadt, there was the equipment because there was a model camp and they had equipment there.
They telegraphed the Russians that if you don't show up by such and such time, so many people will be gassed.
And they did show up.
And they came, and we were not able-- they did not take us for destruction.
And then they saw what, what was happening there, emaciated bodies.
And my skin was brown and my bones.
There was no longer any flesh.
The color of the skin was black.
And they handed the people beans-- bacon and beans in cans.
And people ate it.
And more than half of the camp died of dysentery.
I didn't.
I couldn't stand there.
I couldn't go and grab for it so much, so I got very little.
And I too got dysentery.
But mine-- I didn't eat enough of it.
And so again--
We were together.
And [SIGHS] we couldn't-- the camp couldn't be taken-- after we were freed, we could not be moved because everybody there had dysentery.
There were no-- there was no doctor around to-- there was no medicine around.
The Russians came-- they-- I don't think they expected this kind of a-- of a, human, human destruction.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so they, they gave us all that food from their-- from their rations.
And that killed.
That killed.
And eventually, eventually, we were put on the-- the ones they could clean up, we were put on to buses.
And we were going towards Prague.
And that was a very, very nice thing.
The bus driver pulled up to a cherry orchard.
And he says, eat cherries, whatever you want.
So we helped ourselves to the cherries.
And we went to Prague.
And there the, the UNRRA, the [INAUDIBLE] has helped people.
And we were standing-- my sister and I were standing in front of a building.
And we said, what's now?
Now we have free.
[LAUGHS] We are skin and bone.
We have nothing.
And there comes this gentleman.
And I had a picture of him in gray spats and morning coat with a top hat.
Real-- dressed for a-- the proper way which was at the time when you're a gentleman.
And he says, do you ladies have cigarettes for sale?
Which was the most unlikely questions, but this was his opening statement.
And then we says, no.
We just came from Theresienstadt.
And we still spoke Czech.
And he was very interested in our story.
And then he says, look, I have a friend in the Podolski Sanatorium.
Why don't I take you there and let him examine you?
So he puts us into a cab.
And we were not worried that this gentleman from no place is putting us into a cab.
We went.
Meanwhile, he gave us food.
He fed us.
And we come to this sanatorium.
And his friend looks at us, and he examine-- he puts-- gives us-- me an x-ray.
And my lungs have been infiltrated with TB.
And he says, she goes to the TB section in the sanatorium.
My sister he put into the-- to the-- heart pavilion.
And we were there.
And then started-- and he was our friend.
He came to visit us every day.
This gentleman.
Stanski, Panas Stanski.
And he had a son who has gone off to the circus.
And he was heartbroken.
So--
A Czech.
He was a Czech.
And then, of course, he met the rest of our friends and-- who were also in the hospitals.
Not in mine-- I was the only one in that section because there were other hospitals designated for people who came out of the concentration camps.
And he was just very supportive and loving and caring.
And then we-- I was the 13th in a hospital room.
Everybody had various degrees of impairment.
And they didn't eat much.
And they just pointed.
Take the food to her.
The 12 o'clock meal was sent to me.
I had one meal and then I had 12 others.
Was all lined up around me, around my bed.
And I ate.
I gained five kilos the first week.
And my lungs enjoyed that because they kept on knitting.
I had the calcium injections.
And it helped.
But I gained and gained.
And they, they-- the people who checked me, every time they checked the x-ray-- and they said, look at that stomach.
That stomach is full.
[LAUGHS]
And it, it was-- it was-- now our thoughts were turning towards where are our mother?
Because we didn't-- then we hoped that maybe my mother is alive.
Maybe my sister and her children.
And we were-- we were asking questions.
My brother Moric was there.
And he says, did you see my sisters, my brother?
Who-- and somebody gave him the wrong information that they went to Munkacs.
And he went there, and he got typhoid.
Mhm.
And he couldn't get out.
And he was-- he got stuck there.
Or he was-- after all that, he went to Munkacs to look for us, and he couldn't get out.
Then the Russian border clamped down.
And he was stuck.
That part of--
--Carpathia was closed down.
My brother Jeno came out of Dachau.
And he was bayoneted.
He was hurt.
He was beaten up.
And what they did to the men in there, it was horrible.
And that-- we had a, a-- he had someone in the camp from our hometown, a man who was-- who took him under his wings.
And he saved his life, Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And I'm still friendly with two-- his daughters.
And the-- and his wife is still alive.
She's about 90-something--
--lovely, lovely people.
I just saw one of the girls in Florida.
I said, your father was my brother's protector.
And we are asking-- but there I am, in the sanatorium.
I don't know how to go about-- to go because that was on a hill, way out in the suburbs of Prague-- beautiful place.
And I-- and then one day, I-- this is this very long corridor.
And I am on the other end of that corridor.
And I-- and somebody-- it's on the ground floor.
And I see somebody jumping up and down by the window.
And I go to the window.
And it is Jeno.
I can't jump out the window because it's still too high.
And I start screaming [SOBBING] on the top of my lungs.
[SOBBING] Top of my lungs, I'm screaming, and I'm running to the front through that corridor.
And all those people who were there in the rooms and who could hardly get out of bed, they were all in the-- and the doctors and everyone was running after me.
And I am screaming.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] And there, by the front steps is my brother.
Well-- and he was always my best friend.
He was my protector always.
When somebody-- when I would walk with the-- not very straight, he would say straighten out.
Eat like this.
Don't do that.
And he would always make sure-- very gently.
He was-- very gently.
And he was there.
My friend, my brother.
Well, [SOBBING] that reunion was very, very special.
And I'll be looking for more and looking and looking.
And then this brother who had-- his lungs were very badly affected.
And he got into a-- another hospital by the yacht club in Prague.
There's only one yacht club there at that time.
And he got into that hospital.
And eventually, I was transferred out of that hospital and went where my brother was.
And, and we were together.
And my sister, then, she was discharged.
And she took a job with someone.
And then she came to visit us.
So now we are the three of us.
And we cannot find anybody in there.
And it was a very anxious time to see who knows whom-- looking for whom.
And then my sister goes home to Munkacs to see if she can find my brother.
And she finds him, but he cannot come out because he has typhoid.
I mean, the fever.
He has-- he wasn't able to move.
And here I am in the Prague.
And I'm-- I have-- I need somebody to take care of me.
I'm young, and she is in charge of me.
So she leaves my brother there and goes back to Prague.
And then we still are looking for my mother.
